nameMeanings.name: Lascelles
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: English (of Norman origin): habitational name from one of the large number of places in northern France called Celle, Celles, Lacelle, Leschelle, or Lachelle, all from French celle ‘hermitage’, and at different times occurring both with and without the plural -s.
Variant of   Lacelle   1, a surname of French origin.

variants.name: Laswell
meanings: English: possibly an altered form of   Lascelles  . This surname is also found as   Lacefield  .

variants.name: Lacelle
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  French Alain, Andre, Aurele, Benoit, Cecile, Marcel, Marie Paule.
Altered form of French Laselle: habitational name from La Selle, the name of several places in various parts of France. The surname Laselle is very rare in France, found in Marne. Compare Lascelles .

In some cases possibly also French: habitational name from Lacelle in Corrèze or numerous minor places called La Celle, all named for a monastic cell or hermitage in their vicinity, from French celle ‘hermitage’.

nameMeanings.name: Johanna
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.

variants.name: Jane
meanings: Originally a feminine form of John , from the Old French form Je(h)anne. Since the 17th century it has proved the most popular of the feminine forms of John, ahead of Joan and Jean . It now also commonly occurs as the second element in combinations such as Sarah-Jane. In Britain it is still one of the most frequent of all girls' names. It is not a royal name, but was borne by the tragic Lady Jane Grey ( 1537–54 ), who was unwillingly proclaimed queen in 1553 , deposed nine days later, and executed the following year. Seventy years earlier, the name had come into prominence as that of Jane Shore , mistress of King Edward IV and subsequently of Thomas Grey , 1st Marquess of Dorset, Lady Jane's grandfather. Jane Shore's tribulations in 1483 at the hands of Richard III , Edward's brother and successor, became the subject of popular ballads and plays, which may well have increased the currency of the name in the 16th century. A 19th-century influence was its use as the name of the central character in Charlotte Brontë's novel Jane Eyre ( 1847 ). From 1932 to 1959 it was used as the name of a cheerful and scantily clad beauty whose adventures were chronicled in a strip cartoon in the Daily Mirror. It is also borne by the American film stars Jane Russell ( 1921–2011 ) and Jane Fonda ( b. 1937 ).

en.lifeSummary: Johanna Lascelles was born about 1400, in Lascelles Hall, West Yorkshire, England, United Kingdom. She had at least 1 son with Henry Beaumont. She died after 1447, in Kirkheaton, West Yorkshire, England, United Kingdom, and was buried in St John the Baptist's Church, Kirkheaton, West Yorkshire, England, United Kingdom.
